category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part C/Linear Algebra — Reflection Maps and Inner Products

questiontext.text:
For nonzero \(w\in\mathbb{R}^n\), define the Householder reflection \(T_w(v)=v-2\dfrac{\langle v,w\rangle}{\langle w,w\rangle}w\). Which statements are TRUE?

  1. \(\det(T_w)=-1\).
  2. \(T_w\) preserves the inner product: \(\langle T_w(u),T_w(v)\rangle=\langle u,v\rangle\).
  3. \(T_w^2=I\) (i.e., \(T_w\) is its own inverse).
  4. \(T_{2w}=2T_w\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C)

(A) Householder reflections are orthogonal with \(\det=-1\) (they reverse orientation). (B) \(T_w\) is orthogonal: it preserves inner products. (C) \(T_w^2=I\): applying the reflection twice returns to the original vector. (D) FALSE: \(T_{2w}(v)=v-2\frac{\langle v,2w\rangle}{\langle 2w,2w\rangle}2w=v-2\frac{\langle v,w\rangle}{\langle w,w\rangle}w=T_w(v)\). So \(T_{2w}=T_w\), not \(2T_w\).

questiontext.text:
A linear map \(Q:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n\) is orthogonal if \(Q^TQ=I\). Which are TRUE?

  1. Orthogonal maps preserve the Euclidean norm.
  2. \(\det(Q)=\pm1\).
  3. Rotations and reflections in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) are orthogonal.
  4. The product of two orthogonal matrices is orthogonal.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) \(\|Qv\|^2=v^TQ^TQv=v^Tv=\|v\|^2\). (B) \(\det(Q^TQ)=\det(I)=1\Rightarrow(\det Q)^2=1\). (C) Both are orthogonal (\(\det=1\) for rotations, \(\det=-1\) for reflections). (D) \((Q_1Q_2)^TQ_1Q_2=Q_2^TQ_1^TQ_1Q_2=Q_2^TQ_2=I\).

questiontext.text:
The Gram-Schmidt process applied to a basis \(\{v_1,\ldots,v_n\}\) of an inner product space produces an orthonormal basis \(\{e_1,\ldots,e_n\}\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(\mathrm{span}\{e_1,\ldots,e_k\}=\mathrm{span}\{v_1,\ldots,v_k\}\) for each \(k\).
  2. The process always terminates successfully when the \(v_i\) are linearly independent.
  3. The resulting \(\{e_i\}\) satisfies \(\langle e_i,e_j\rangle=\delta_{ij}\).
  4. The process can be used to produce the QR decomposition of a matrix.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) At each step we only take linear combinations within the span. (B) Linear independence guarantees non-zero vectors at each step. (C) Orthonormality by construction. (D) QR: write the original basis as columns of \(A\); Gram-Schmidt gives \(A=QR\) with \(Q\) orthogonal and \(R\) upper triangular.

questiontext.text:
The spectral theorem for real symmetric matrices states: if \(A=A^T\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\), then:

  1. All eigenvalues of \(A\) are real.
  2. Eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal.
  3. \(A\) is orthogonally diagonalizable: \(A=Q\Lambda Q^T\) with \(Q\) orthogonal.
  4. \(A\) has at least one positive eigenvalue.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C)

(A)(B)(C) are the spectral theorem. (D) FALSE: \(A=-I\) is symmetric with all eigenvalues \(-1<0\).

questiontext.text:
An inner product on a real vector space \(V\) satisfies: for all \(u,v,w\in V\) and \(c\in\mathbb{R}\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(\langle u,v\rangle=\langle v,u\rangle\) (symmetry).
  2. \(\langle cu+v,w\rangle=c\langle u,w\rangle+\langle v,w\rangle\) (linearity in first argument).
  3. \(\langle v,v\rangle\ge0\) with equality iff \(v=0\).
  4. \(\langle u,cv\rangle=c\langle u,v\rangle\) (linearity in second argument, for real inner products).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

All four are properties of a real inner product: (A) symmetry, (B) linearity in first argument, (C) positive definiteness, (D) linearity in second argument (follows from (A) and (B) in the real case).

questiontext.text:
The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality: \(|\langle u,v\rangle|\le\|u\|\|v\|\). Which statements follow from Cauchy-Schwarz?

  1. The angle between two nonzero vectors \(\theta\) satisfies \(|\cos\theta|\le1\).
  2. The triangle inequality: \(\|u+v\|\le\|u\|+\|v\|\).
  3. Equality holds iff \(u\) and \(v\) are linearly dependent.
  4. In \(\mathbb{R}^n\): \(\left(\sum a_ib_i\right)^2\le\left(\sum a_i^2\right)\left(\sum b_i^2\right)\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) \(\cos\theta=\langle u,v\rangle/(\|u\|\|v\|)\), and Cauchy-Schwarz gives \(|\cos\theta|\le1\). (B) Triangle inequality follows from \(\|u+v\|^2=\|u\|^2+2\langle u,v\rangle+\|v\|^2\le(\|u\|+\|v\|)^2\). (C) Equality: \(u=cv\) for some scalar. (D) Cauchy-Schwarz for \(\mathbb{R}^n\) with standard inner product.

questiontext.text:
Let \(Q\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\) be an orthogonal matrix. Which are TRUE?

  1. All eigenvalues of \(Q\) have absolute value 1.
  2. Real eigenvalues of \(Q\) are \(\pm1\).
  3. Complex eigenvalues of \(Q\) come in conjugate pairs.
  4. If \(n\) is odd, \(Q\) always has at least one real eigenvalue.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) If \(Qv=\lambda v\): \(\|Qv\|=\|v\|\Rightarrow|\lambda|=1\). (B) Real eigenvalues satisfy \(|\lambda|=1\Rightarrow\lambda=\pm1\). (C) Real matrices have complex eigenvalues in conjugate pairs. (D) For odd \(n\), the characteristic polynomial (degree \(n\), real coefficients) must have at least one real root.

questiontext.text:
A linear operator \(T\) on an inner product space is self-adjoint if \(T^*=T\) (where \(T^*\) is the adjoint). Which are TRUE?

  1. All eigenvalues of a self-adjoint operator are real.
  2. Eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues of a self-adjoint operator are orthogonal.
  3. A self-adjoint operator is always diagonalizable (in finite dimensions).
  4. \(\langle Tu,v\rangle=\langle u,Tv\rangle\) for all \(u,v\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A)(B)(C) Spectral theorem for self-adjoint operators. (D) This is the definition of self-adjoint.

questiontext.text:
The singular value decomposition (SVD) of \(A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}\) is \(A=U\Sigma V^T\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(U\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times m}\) and \(V\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\) are orthogonal matrices.
  2. The diagonal entries of \(\Sigma\) (singular values) are non-negative.
  3. The number of nonzero singular values equals \(\mathrm{rank}(A)\).
  4. The columns of \(V\) are eigenvectors of \(A^TA\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) \(U,V\) are orthogonal. (B) Singular values \(\sigma_i\ge0\). (C) \(\mathrm{rank}(A)=\)number of nonzero \(\sigma_i\). (D) \(A^TAv_i=\sigma_i^2v_i\), so columns of \(V\) are eigenvectors of \(A^TA\).

questiontext.text:
Let \(\{e_k\}_{k=1}^n\) be an orthonormal set in an inner product space \(V\), and \(v\in V\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(\sum_{k=1}^n|\langle v,e_k\rangle|^2\le\|v\|^2\) (Bessel's inequality).
  2. Equality holds iff \(v\in\mathrm{span}\{e_1,\ldots,e_n\}\).
  3. The best approximation of \(v\) from \(\mathrm{span}\{e_k\}\) is \(\hat{v}=\sum\langle v,e_k\rangle e_k\).
  4. \(\|v-\hat{v}\|^2=\|v\|^2-\sum|\langle v,e_k\rangle|^2\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Bessel's inequality. (B) Parseval: equality iff \(v\) lies in the span. (C) The orthogonal projection onto the span. (D) Pythagorean theorem applied to \(v=\hat{v}+(v-\hat{v})\) with \(\hat{v}\perp(v-\hat{v})\).

questiontext.text:
A symmetric matrix \(A\) is positive definite (PD) if \(x^TAx>0\) for all \(x\ne0\). Which are equivalent to PD?

  1. All eigenvalues of \(A\) are positive.
  2. All leading principal minors are positive (Sylvester's criterion).
  3. \(A=B^TB\) for some invertible \(B\) (Cholesky).
  4. \(A\) has a unique Cholesky decomposition \(A=LL^T\) with \(L\) lower triangular and positive diagonal.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

All four are equivalent characterizations of a symmetric positive definite matrix. (A) Spectral criterion. (B) Sylvester's criterion. (C) PD iff it can be written as \(B^TB\) with invertible \(B\). (D) The Cholesky decomposition exists and is unique for PD matrices.

questiontext.text:
A complex matrix \(U\in\mathbb{C}^{n\times n}\) is unitary if \(U^*U=I\). Which are TRUE?

  1. Unitary matrices preserve the Hermitian inner product.
  2. Eigenvalues of unitary matrices lie on the unit circle \(|\lambda|=1\).
  3. Unitary matrices are diagonalizable over \(\mathbb{C}\).
  4. The product of two unitary matrices is unitary.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) \(\langle Uu,Uv\rangle=v^*U^*Uu=v^*u=\langle u,v\rangle\). (B) If \(Uv=\lambda v\): \(\|Uv\|=\|v\|\Rightarrow|\lambda|=1\). (C) By the spectral theorem for normal matrices (\(UU^*=U^*U\)). (D) \((U_1U_2)^*U_1U_2=U_2^*U_1^*U_1U_2=U_2^*U_2=I\).

questiontext.text:
For \(T\in\mathcal{L}(V,W)\) (finite-dimensional inner product spaces), the adjoint \(T^*:W\to V\) satisfies \(\langle Tv,w\rangle_W=\langle v,T^*w\rangle_V\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \((T^*)^*=T\).
  2. \((ST)^*=T^*S^*\).
  3. \(\mathrm{rank}(T^*)=\mathrm{rank}(T)\).
  4. \(\ker(T^*)=(\mathrm{Range}(T))^\perp\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Double adjoint. (B) Adjoint reverses order. (C) Rank is preserved by taking adjoint. (D) Fundamental relation: \(\ker(T^*)=\mathrm{Range}(T)^\perp\).

questiontext.text:
The rotation matrix in \(\mathbb{R}^2\): \(R(\theta)=\begin{pmatrix}\cos\theta&-\sin\theta\\\sin\theta&\cos\theta\end{pmatrix}\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(R(\theta)^T=R(-\theta)\).
  2. \(R(\theta_1)R(\theta_2)=R(\theta_1+\theta_2)\).
  3. \(\det(R(\theta))=1\).
  4. \(R(\theta)\) has eigenvalues \(e^{\pm i\theta}\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Rotation by \(-\theta\) is the transpose. (B) Angle addition formula. (C) \(\cos^2\theta+\sin^2\theta=1\). (D) Characteristic poly: \(\lambda^2-2\cos\theta\lambda+1=0\Rightarrow\lambda=e^{\pm i\theta}\).

questiontext.text:
For a subspace \(W\) of a finite-dimensional inner product space \(V\), which are TRUE?

  1. \(V=W\oplus W^\perp\).
  2. \((W^\perp)^\perp=W\).
  3. \(\dim W+\dim W^\perp=\dim V\).
  4. The orthogonal projection \(P_W\) satisfies \(P_W^2=P_W\) and \(P_W^*=P_W\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

All four are standard properties of orthogonal complements in finite-dimensional inner product spaces. (A) Orthogonal direct sum decomposition. (B) Reflexivity of orthogonal complement in finite dimensions. (C) Dimension count. (D) Orthogonal projections are self-adjoint idempotents.

questiontext.text:
A quadratic form \(Q(x)=x^TAx\) with \(A\) symmetric. Which are TRUE?

  1. Every quadratic form can be diagonalized via an orthogonal change of variables.
  2. A quadratic form is PD iff all eigenvalues of \(A\) are positive.
  3. The signature \((p,q)\) of a quadratic form is the number of positive and negative eigenvalues respectively (Sylvester's law).
  4. If \(Q(x)=x_1^2+2x_1x_2+x_2^2=(x_1+x_2)^2\), then \(Q\) is positive semidefinite.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Spectral theorem allows orthogonal diagonalization of any symmetric \(A\). (B) PD iff all eigenvalues positive. (C) Sylvester's law of inertia: the signature is invariant under congruence transformations. (D) \(Q(x)=(x_1+x_2)^2\ge0\) with equality at \(x_1=-x_2\ne0\): positive semidefinite (not positive definite).

questiontext.text:
An operator \(T\) on a complex inner product space is normal if \(TT^*=T^*T\). Which are TRUE?

  1. Self-adjoint operators are normal.
  2. Unitary operators are normal.
  3. Normal operators are diagonalizable by a unitary matrix.
  4. If \(T\) is normal and \(Tv=\lambda v\), then \(T^*v=\bar\lambda v\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Self-adjoint: \(TT^*=T^2=T^*T\). (B) Unitary: \(UU^*=I=U^*U\). (C) Spectral theorem for normal operators on complex spaces. (D) For normal \(T\): \(\|Tv-\lambda v\|=\|(T-\lambda I)v\|=\|(T-\lambda I)^*v\|=\|(T^*-\bar\lambda I)v\|\Rightarrow T^*v=\bar\lambda v\).

questiontext.text:
Householder reflections are used in numerical QR decomposition. Which are TRUE?

  1. A Householder reflection can zero out all entries below the pivot in a column.
  2. The product of Householder matrices is orthogonal.
  3. Householder QR is numerically more stable than Gram-Schmidt.
  4. Each Householder reflection takes \(O(n^2)\) operations to apply to an \(n\times n\) matrix.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) A Householder reflection \(H_k\) maps a vector to a multiple of a standard basis vector, zeroing the desired entries. (B) Products of orthogonal matrices are orthogonal. (C) Householder QR is considered numerically more stable. (D) Applying \(H=I-2vv^T\) to a matrix: \(HA=A-2v(v^TA)\) costs \(O(n^2)\).

questiontext.text:
A symmetric matrix \(A\) has spectral decomposition \(A=\sum_{i=1}^n\lambda_i e_ie_i^T\). Which are TRUE?

  1. \(A^k=\sum\lambda_i^k e_ie_i^T\) for all integers \(k\) (when \(A\) is invertible for negative \(k\)).
  2. The pseudoinverse is \(A^+=\sum_{\lambda_i\ne0}\lambda_i^{-1}e_ie_i^T\).
  3. \(\mathrm{tr}(A)=\sum\lambda_i\).
  4. \(\det(A)=\prod\lambda_i\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

All four follow from the spectral decomposition. (A) Matrix functions via spectral decomposition. (B) Pseudoinverse: invert nonzero eigenvalues. (C) Trace = sum of eigenvalues. (D) Determinant = product of eigenvalues.

questiontext.text:
In an inner product space with complete orthonormal system \(\{e_k\}\), Parseval's identity states \(\|v\|^2=\sum_k|\langle v,e_k\rangle|^2\). Which follow?

  1. Every \(v\) can be written as \(v=\sum_k\langle v,e_k\rangle e_k\).
  2. \(\langle u,v\rangle=\sum_k\langle u,e_k\rangle\overline{\langle v,e_k\rangle}\).
  3. The map \(v\mapsto(\langle v,e_k\rangle)\) is an isometric isomorphism to \(\ell^2\).
  4. In \(L^2([0,2\pi])\), the exponentials \(e^{inx}/\sqrt{2\pi}\) form a complete orthonormal system.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D)

(A) Completeness of the orthonormal system. (B) Polarization identity applied to Parseval. (C) This is an isometric isomorphism to \(\ell^2\) (abstract Riesz-Fischer). (D) The Fourier system is complete in \(L^2([0,2\pi])\) — classical result.
